IET Circuits, Devices & Systems  
Research Article  
ISSN 1751-858X  
Adjacency criticality: a simple yet effective  
metric for statistical timing yield optimisation  
of digital integrated circuits  
Received on 24th December 2018  
Revised 3rd April 2019  
Accepted on 7th May 2019  
E-First on 22nd October 2019  
doi: 10.1049/iet-cds.2018.5616  
www.ietdl.org  
Seyed Milad Ebrahimipour1, Behnam Ghavami1 , Mohsen Raji2  
1Department of Computer Engineering, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran  
2School of Electrical & Computer Engineering, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran  
E-mail: ghavami@uk.ac.ir  
Abstract: As CMOS devices become smaller, process variations-induced uncertainty imposes a large spread in the circuit  
timing and therefore, it becomes one of the main issues for circuit yield. To analyse/optimise the timing of the circuit under  
process variation effects, statistical analysis/optimisation techniques are more suitable than the traditional static analysis/  
optimisation counterparts. Statistical gate sizing is an effective technique that is widely used to guide the timing yield  
improvement of digital circuits. Gate criticality, defined as the probability that a gate lies on a critical path, forms the basis for  
many of the existing statistical gate sizing techniques. Here, the authors introduce adjacency criticality to address the  
drawbacks of the conventional definition of gate criticality. It is defined as the probability of manufacturing a chip in which the  
gate lies on the critical path due to process variation considering the effect of the gates in its fan-out cone. Furthermore, the  
authors present the levelised Adjacency Criticality metric which provides a trade-off between the runtime of the criticality metric  
and accuracy of the Adjacency Criticality metric. In order to show the efficacy of the proposed metric, an adjacency criticality-  
based statistical gate sizing method is presented for improving timing yield of the circuit.  
SSTAs as well as the information provided by the circuit analyser  
to be applied in the optimisation method [13].  
1ꢀIntroduction  
In order to gain more advances in different aspects such as  
performance and power consumption, integrated circuits  
manufacturing continuously has followed a trend of transistor  
dimensions shrinking [1]. As technology continues to scale down,  
the difficulty of precise fabrication process increases. This causes  
fabrication process-induced parameter variations (briefly process  
variations) such as changes in gate oxide thickness and threshold  
voltage which significantly affect the circuit timing and  
performance [2]. Process variations may change the circuit  
frequency up to 30% [3]. Thus, the gap between the designed  
layout and what is really fabricated on silicon is widening  
significantly. Handling the process variations effects at design time  
has the advantage of taking the right decisions early in the design  
flow which in turn, significantly reduces the time-to-market of the  
design. As a result, design-time compensating of process variations  
impacts is an inevitable requirement for modern digital circuits.  
Deterministic timing analysis has been widely used to handle  
variability effects in very large-scale integrated (VLSI) circuit  
designs [4, 5]. However, this approach loses accuracy in analysing  
circuits under considerable process variations in nanoscale era.  
This is due to the fact that it estimates nominal, best-case, or worst-  
case delay of a circuit; best-case (worst-case) analysis significantly  
underestimates (overestimates) the process variations effects, and  
analysing circuits at nominal values of various circuit parameters  
completely ignores these variations [4, 5]. As a promising  
alternative, statistical analysis which is based on estimating the  
distribution performance of the circuit metrics using probability  
theory and statistics has shown more accurate results in variability-  
aware performance evaluation of digital circuits. Statistical static  
timing analysis (SSTA) is a widely used method for timing analysis  
in digital circuits in the presence of process variations. There are  
many SSTA methods that trade-off between time complexity and  
accuracy [6–11]. Statistical timing optimisation methods take  
advantages of SSTAs as the basis in order to improve the  
performance/yield of a digital circuit considering the impacts of  
process variations [12]. Hence, the efficacy of the statistical timing  
optimisation method is directly affected by the quality of the  
In order to obtain specialised information for steering statistical  
optimisation, characteristic metrics have been introduced [6, 13–  
35]. Yield gradient (YG) which is an effective sensitivity metric for  
guiding the statistical timing optimisation flow is defined as the  
change in the timing yield (TY) of the circuit for small changes in  
the size of the gates [19, 24]. To assess the change in TY of the  
circuit, an SSTA is performed to obtain the change in the circuit  
delay due to a change in the size of the gates; i.e. YG is evaluated  
numerically. In [24], using a YG computation method, a gate sizing  
algorithm for parametric yield maximisation is introduced.  
Although YG is an accurate metric, YG-based optimisation  
algorithms are intrinsically iterative, and in each iteration, the YG  
of a large number of gates needs to be evaluated in order to  
designate the best candidate for resizing. Another metric, TY slack  
(YS) is introduced in [13]. The YS represents the extra TY  
available in a gate or a circuit. However, in order to compute YS of  
each gate, a large number of gates needs to be evaluated which is  
time-consuming for even mid-scale digital circuits.  
In [20, 22], an indicator named as statistical timing criticality  
(STC) is introduced that represents the probability that the gate lies  
on the critical path considering process variations. STC is used for  
TY optimisation by improving the delay of the gates which have  
high STC values. In [25], the criticality of a path is expressed as  
the sensitivity of the mean of the normally distributed random  
variable of circuit delay to the mean of the path delay. In [6], a fast  
method to compute the criticality of the path and the node/arc is  
presented. This method is valid under the assumption that the paths  
are independent. However, due to correlated parameters and path  
reconvergence, it is not sufficiently accurate to be applied for TY  
optimisation of large and complicated circuits. Zhan et al. [26]  
compute the path criticality based on max operations and Monte–  
Carlo simulations. In this method, using a pre-selected path set,  
paths are picked out for criticality computation. However, this  
method suffers from a serious drawback, i.e. it is not clear how to  
determine the pre-selected path set (like path-based SSTA). Xiong  
et al. [27] introduced a cut-set-based method to compute criticality.  
After identifying all the cut set of the timing graph, the node/edge  
criticality is defined as the probability of the node/edge slack (the  
IET Circuits Devices Syst., 2019, Vol. 13 Iss. 7, pp. 979-987  
979  
© The Institution of Engineering and Technology 2019  
maximum delay of all paths going through that node/edge) being  
greater than or equal to the complementary node/edge slack, i.e. the  
maximum delay of all paths which are not going through that node/  
edge. In order to overcome the error induced by max operator in  
criticality computation, a criticality computation method using  
unary and conditional operator is introduced in [28]. In [22, 29], an  
incremental criticality computation using circuit delay is  
introduced. In order to reduce the power consumption of the  
circuit, [31, 32] propose a dual supply voltage (DSV) technique in  
which a lower supply voltage is applied to gates such that the  
performance of the circuit maintains at the same time. To this end,  
these methods first compute the criticality of each gate. Then, DSV  
is applied to a group of the gates with the lowest criticality. At the  
presence of false paths, i.e. paths through which signals cannot  
propagate through it, finding the true critical paths is so difficult. If  
the false paths are the critical paths of the circuit, analysis of the  
circuit using SSTA would be too optimistic. Furthermore,  
criticality computation of the circuit would be inaccurate. Sreenath  
and Ramesh [33] propose a gate sizing method in which they first  
identify the false path of the circuit. Then, a group of gates selected  
from the true critical paths according to their criticality and gate  
sizing is applied to them.  
In order to lessen the impact of process variation, post-silicon  
clock tuning elements are widely used in high-performance  
circuits. These elements can be configured through the scan chain  
so that clock skews to these flip-flops can be adjusted after  
manufacturing. Moreover, since these elements allow the path  
delays to compensate each other across flip-flops, critical paths in  
such circuits may span more than one stage which makes the  
criticality computation of such a circuit so challenging. In [34], a  
SSTA method is introduced to compute the performance of the  
circuit under post-silicon clock tuning elements. Furthermore, they  
propose a criticality computation method in these circuits so that an  
analysing-optimisation flow becomes possible.  
• Arrival Time (AT):‘the time in which the data arrives at the  
internal node’[5]. It incorporates all the net and logic delays  
between the reference input point and the destination node.  
In order to compute the delay distribution of the circuit, timing  
graph of the circuit is created. The timing graph is a directed  
acyclic graph G(V, E) composed of nodes (V) and edges (E). The  
nodes represent different input pins of circuit gates. They are  
connected using edges that model signal propagation from one  
node to another with an associated delay demonstrating either gate  
or wire delay. Considering PV effects, this delay is a random  
variable with a specific probability density function (PDF). The  
timing graph is augmented by a single source node and a single  
sink node. The source node is a virtual node which is connected to  
all primary inputs via virtual edges and the virtual sink node is a  
virtual node which is connected to all primary outputs or timing  
test nodes with virtual edges.  
Given the circuit timing graph and the statistical gate delay  
model as inputs, SSTA computes the statistical distribution of the  
circuit delay with linear runtime complexity [11]. In this paper, we  
apply the widely used canonical first-order delay model [6] with a  
relatively low complexity. The delay of a gate under PV effects  
considering spatial correlation can be stated as follows [33]:

the sensitivity of the delay to each of the random variables, and d0

Generally, SSTA consists of two repeating steps:  
In order to optimise the delay of the circuit, critical paths or  
nodes are the first targets in applying the circuit optimisation  
technique. Thus, it is important to select the critical gates correctly.  
However, all of the previous optimisation methods using criticality  
are suffering from a serious drawback. They did not consider the  
effect of the gates in the fan-out cone of each gate. Since  
optimising the delay of gate g will affect the arrival time (AT) of  
the gates in its fan-out cone, and as the criticality of the gates in the  
fan-out cone of a gate become higher, the effect of optimising that  
gate on the circuit delay become higher, neglecting the effect of the  
fan-out gates will give a more pessimistic (optimistic) criticality  
which slows down the convergence rate of the optimisation  
algorithm and result in high area overhead.  
Step(1) Arrival Time Propagation: in this step, the AT propagates  
from the gate input to the gate output.  
Step(2) Merging Arrival Times: this step merges multiple ATs  
converging them into the gate output from different gate inputs by  
calculating the maximum of the ATs.  
2.1 Arrival time propagation  
In this step, the gate delay is added to the AT. If ATgate and Dgate  
,

respectively, represent the AT and delay of a gate in the canonical  
form, we have:  
In this paper, we propose a novel optimisation metric called  
adjacency criticality (AC). It is defined as the probability of  
manufacturing a chip in which the gate lies on the critical path, due  
to process variation considering the effect of gates in its fan-out  
cone. In order to show the efficacy of the proposed metric, an AC-  
based statistical gate sizing method is presented for improving TY  
of the circuit. Experimental results on EPFL and ISCAS  
benchmark circuits show that the proposed metric leads to faster  
convergence of the optimisation algorithm and lower area overhead  
compared to the criticality metric.

The rest of this paper is organised as follows. Section2 provides  
an overview of the gate-level delay model and the SSTA used in  
this paper. In Section 3, we motivate the AC metric and the  
proposed algorithm to calculate AC of each gate is presented. In  
Section 4, the proposed gate sizing method to improve the TY of  
the circuit is introduced. Section5 reports the experimental results.  
In the end, the paper is concluded in Section 6.

2ꢀPreliminaries: SSTA  
2.2 Merging arrival times  
Before explaining about SSTA, there are some parameters needed

for computing a statistical approximation of the maximum of two

Fig. 2ꢁ Combinational circuit with associated adjacency criticality  
example, consider the combinational circuit shown in Fig. 1. Each  
gate of the circuit is annotated with its criticality. The criticalities  
in this example are computed using the method proposed in [28].  
As it can be seen, gate g3 has the largest criticality and it will be  
the first candidate of the optimisation algorithm. However, if we  
consider the effect of the gates in the fan-out cone of each gate,  
gate g2 will be chosen for optimisation because g3 and g4 which  
are located in the fan-out cone of g2 have the largest criticalities  
and optimising g2 will affect the delay of the paths going through  
g3 and g4. As a result, it leads to a faster optimisation process.  
All of the optimisation methods using criticality are suffering  
from a serious drawback; i.e. they did not consider the effect of the  
gates in the fan-out cone of each gate. As the criticality of the gates  
in the fan-out cone of g becomes higher, the effect of optimising g  
on the circuit delay increases. Therefore, we need to select a gate  
that not only has a high criticality but also the criticality of the  
gates in its fan-out cone is high.

Using this intuition, we define AC concept. The AC of a gate is  
defined as the probability of that the gate lies on the critical path  
considering the effect of gates in its fan-out cone. In order to  
compute AC of each gate, the average of the criticality of the gates  
in the fan-out cone of that gate is calculated. The AC of gatei is

3ꢀAdjacency criticality  
expressed as:  
Due to process variation effects, path delays are no longer  
deterministic values, but are described by random variable with a  
specific PDF. This implies that each of the paths can be critical  
with some probability, and none of the paths can be critical with  
absolute certainty; i.e. we have a set of critical paths instead of  
only a critical path. Since the critical path set changes from die to  
die due to process variations effects, computing criticality is a  
crucial step in TY optimisation. As defined in [21], ‘Criticality of a  
path is the probability of manufacturing a chip in which the path of  
interest is critical’. ‘Criticality of a gate is defined as the  
probability of manufacturing a chip in which the gate lies on the  
critical path, due to process variation’[21]. In other words, the  
criticality of a gate is the criticality of the set of all paths going  
through that gate. Using criticality, critical paths or gates become  
the key nominees to apply the circuit optimisation techniques [27].  
Thus, in order to provide noteworthy information for guiding  
statistical optimisation, it is important to accurately compute the  
criticality of each gate. Since optimising the delay of gate g will  
affect the AT of the gates in its fan-out cone, neglecting the effect  
of the fan-out gates will lead to an inaccurate criticality which  
slows down the convergence rate of the optimisation algorithm. For  
ci + ∑nj = 1 cj

1 + n  
where ci is the criticality of gatei and n is the number of gates in the  
fan-out cone of gatei. Fig. 2 shows the AC of each gate computed  
using (15) for the given combinational circuit. As expected, using  
the proposed equation, gate g2 has the largest AC and will be the  
first candidate for the optimisation algorithm.  
Fig. 3 (See Algorithm 1) aims to compute ACgate for each gate  
i
of the circuit using (15). The circuit and the SSTA results are the  
inputs to this algorithm. At first, the criticality of each gate is  
computed (line 7, 8). Then, each gate of the circuit is traversed  
(line 10). During each traversing, the average criticality of the  
gates in fan-out cone of each gate is computed (line 11–21). If the  
gate is an output gate (a gate with no predecessor), the average  
criticality of this gate is equal to its criticality (line 11–15). The  
average criticalities of other gates are computed by calculating the  
average of the criticality of their predecessors (line 16–19). Finally,

Fig. 5ꢁ Algorithm 3. Gate Sizing Algorithm  
where cj. l and kj represent criticality of lth gate and number of  
gates in level j, respectively.  
Fig. 3ꢁ Algorithm 1: Adjacency Criticality Computation  
The overall algorithm to compute AC considering some levels  
of fan-out cone is presented in Fig. 4 (See Algorithm 2). At first,  
the criticality of each gate is computed (line 7, 8). Then, each gate  
is visited in the topological order (line 10). During each traversing,  
the average criticality of the gates in fan-out cone of each gate is  
computed (line 11–17). The average criticality of each gate is  
computed by calculating the average of the criticality of the gates  
in some levels, i.e. one or two levels, of its fan-out cone (line 11–  
15). Finally, using the average criticality of fan-out cone of each  
gate, the AC of each gate is computed (line 16).  
In order to compute AC, we need to compute the criticality of  
each gate. Without loss of generality, the incremental criticality  
computation method introduced in [21, 28] is used to find the  
criticality of each gate. However, any of the similar methods can be  
adapted to the proposed algorithm.  
Incremental timing analysis is a corner-stone of optimisation  
and physical synthesis, in which the timing quantities can be  
updated incrementally after one or more changes are made to the  
circuit. In order to compute the criticality of node v ∈ V  
incrementally, all the paths from the source node to the sink node  
are divided into two disjoint sets. The first set comprises the paths  
going through node v, while the second one comprises the paths  
which are not passing through node v. The maximum delay of  
paths going through v is node slack A which is expressed as:  
A = ATv − RATv  
(17)  
Fig. 4ꢁ Algorithm 2: Levelised Adjacency Criticality Computation  
where ATv and RATv, respectively, represent the AT and required  
AT of node v.  
The maximum delay of paths not going through v is called  
complement node slack B. The node criticality is the probability of  
node slack A being greater than or equal to the complement node  
slack B, i.e.:  
using the average criticality of fan-out cone of each gate, the AC of  
each gate is computed (line 20).  
Computing AC of each gate using (15) is time-consuming  
because it is required to traverse the fan-out cone of each gate to  
find its AC. As expressed in [29–31], the impact of changes in the  
delay of a gate (by a gate resizing) decreases (approximately  
geometrically) with increasing fan-out level. Therefore, in order to  
compute the AC of each gate, it is only required to consider the  
effect of the changes in the gate delay in a few levels of its fan-out  
cone. The question is, for a reasonable accuracy, how many levels  
of fan-out are required to evaluate. Choosing k (number of levels)  
is a trade-off between runtime and accuracy. As a result, The AC of  
gatei can be expressed as:  
t = P A ≥ B  
(18)

t = P A ≥ B = P A ≥ A & A ≥ B = P(A ≥ m ax(AB))

of all paths from the source to the sink or simply AT of the sink

5ꢀExperimental results  
The proposed algorithm of AC computation is implemented in C++  
and run on a Microsoft Windows machine with an Intel Core i7  
(2.4ꢁGHz) processor and an 8ꢁGB of RAM circuit. All of the gates  
in the circuit are assigned with a base size, i.e. the W/L ratio of  
PMOS in an inverter is set to 2 and W/L ratio for its NMOS is set  
to 1. In addition, for other gates (NAND, NOR etc.), we have sized  
the gates such that they have pull-up and pull-down networks with  
equal current strengths. In this paper, we assume process variations  
in effective channel length and threshold voltage. However, one  
could consider other source of variations; for example in the  
channel width and oxide thickness of the transistor. All of the  
random variables are set to the standard Gaussian random variable  
Nꢀ∼ꢀ(0, 1).  
where ATsink represents AT of the sink node.  
Therefore, to update node criticality t, we only need to update  
ATv, RATv and ATsink. In order to do this efficiently and  
incrementally, it is just required to run the SSTA for the fan-in cone  
and fan-out cone of node v. In fact, after incrementally updating  
these quantities, computing gate criticality using (20) can be done  
in a constant time [21].  
4ꢀTiming yield optimisation using adjacency  
criticality  
In this section, we introduce a statistical gate sizing algorithm for  
TY optimisation using AC. Gate sizing is one of the simplest yet  
effective methods for timing optimisation. The TY is the number of  
fabricated chips whose delay is at or below a specified value called  
the timing constraint. In other words, ‘the timing yield of the circuit  
denotes the probability that the circuit slack is non-negative’[36].  
This probability can be computed by integrating the circuit slack  
PDF from 0 to ∞. Given the circuit slack as a Gaussian-distributed  
random variable with mean μS and standard deviation σS, the TYof  
The experiments are divided into two parts. At first, we  
compare the AC concept with the criticality concept using various  
constraints. Then, in the second part, we investigate the efficiency  
of the proposed gate sizing optimisation algorithm using AC  
concept by applying the algorithm to some benchmark circuits.  
5.1 Comparison of AC concept with criticality concept  
the circuit which we try to improve is given by [36]:  
In order to evaluate the efficiency of AC for guiding statistical  
optimisation, we compare the obtained results of improving the  
delay of the circuit using the gate sizing based on the AC and  
conventional definition of the gate criticality introduced in [21, 28].  
The gate sizing algorithm applied for 15 iterations and in each  
iteration, 10% of the gates are picked out from the candidate gate  
set given their optimising priorities. The effectiveness of each  
metric is assessed based on the improvement achieved in the circuit  
delay. Table 1 shows the obtained results. In Table 1, the first seven  
rows are related to ISCAS'85 benchmark circuits and the other  
rows show some circuits from EPFL benchmark suit. In addition,  
columns named as ‘% μ’ and ‘% σ2’, respectively, represent the  
percentage of the mean and variance relative improvement  
provided by the gate sizing. As reported in this table, on average,  
the AC-based gate sizing improves the mean of the circuit by  
23.9% which is >9.73% mean improvement achieved when using  
the criticality-based gate sizing. The AC-based gate sizing method  
also improves the variance of the circuit by 40.57% which is  
>16.93% variance improvement obtained by criticality-based gate  
sizing. The comparison demonstrates that AC is an effective metric  
for guiding statistical optimisation which leads to a better circuit  
delay (both in mean and variance) in comparison with the  
∞

/2σS2

TY =

)dx ≡ ϕ

0
where ϕ( ⋅ ) represents the standard normal cumulative distribution  
function.  
It is worthy to note that, as the yield of the circuit is improved,  
the probability of having a circuit with a delay smaller than or  
equal to the timing constraint increases.  
Fig. 5 (See Algorithm 3) shows the overall gate sizing  
algorithm.  
After the AC of each gate in the circuit is computed, since the  
effect of updating different gates are different and interdependent,  
it is important to select a group of the most effective gates in each  
iteration of the optimisation algorithm. As a result, the gates are  
ranked according to their AC and a group of the highest-ranking  
gates is chosen for optimisation. After each iteration of  
optimisation, SSTA is carried out to obtain the timing of the circuit.  
It is notable that, in the SSTA, only fan-in gates of the n modified  
gates and those on their fan-out cones are required to be re-  
examined. This iterative sizing procedure terminates when the  
IET Circuits Devices Syst., 2019, Vol. 13 Iss. 7, pp. 979-987  
983  
© The Institution of Engineering and Technology 2019  
   
Fig. 6ꢁ Comparison of the delay distribution of the circuit before and after optimisation using AC and criticality for C499, C1908, Arbiter, and Voter circuit  
slope of this reduction (both in the mean and variance) is also more  
than that one in the figure shown for criticality-based optimisation  
method.  
To show the efficiency of AC concept, the proposed AC-based  
optimisation algorithm is compared with the criticality-based  
optimisation method considering different area overhead  
constraints. The proposed method is applied for two different cases  
in which the area overhead is limited to 10 and 20% of the initial  
circuit area. Figs. 9 and 10 compare the obtained results for the  
circuit delay improvement before and after optimisation based on  
AC, levelised AC, and criticality metric. Here, the delay of the  
circuit is computed and reported as μ + 3σ of the circuit delay  
distribution. As it can be seen, in the same area constraint, the  
delay improvement of the circuit optimised with AC is greater than  
the delay of the circuit optimised with criticality; i.e. with a  
specific area overhead, AC-guided optimisation leads to a circuit  
with higher TY than criticality-based optimisation algorithm. The  
delay optimisation using levelised AC varies between AC and  
criticality concept. As the number of fan-out levels increases, the  
delay improvement will be closer to the AC delay improvement.  
In order to investigate the runtime of the optimisation  
algorithm, we compare the runtime of the AC-guided optimisation  
algorithm with the algorithm based on the criticality metrics.  
Table 2 reports the obtained results. In this table, columns named  
as ‘% μ’ and ‘% σ2’, respectively, represent the percentage of the  
mean and variance average relative improvement provided by the  
gate sizing, in each iteration. In addition, column ‘RTPI’ denotes  
the algorithm runtime per iteration, i.e. how long each iteration  
takes. As the results show, due to higher computation time required  
for AC calculation, the runtime of each iteration of AC-based  
optimisation algorithm is on average 3.85×more than the runtime  
of each iteration of criticality-based optimisation algorithm  
(723.11(s) compared to 187.87(s)). Despite this disadvantage, each  
iteration of AC-based gate sizing improves the mean of the circuit  
by 1.59% which is >0.64% mean improvement achieved in each  
iteration of the criticality-based gate sizing optimisation. Each  
iteration of the AC-based gate sizing method also improves the  
variance of circuit 2.54% which is >1.12% variance improvement  
obtained in each iteration of criticality-based gate sizing.  
Fig. 7ꢁ Delay distribution of C499 benchmark circuit after each iteration of  
optimisation using criticality  
conventional gate criticality metric. This table also shows the  
results for levelised AC under different k, i.e. number of fan-out  
levels. As it can be seen, for kꢀ=ꢀ1, the results are close to the  
criticality-based gate sizing results. However, for kꢀ=ꢀ3, the results  
are closer to the results obtained from the AC-based gate sizing as  
compared to kꢀ=ꢀ1 and kꢀ=ꢀ2.  
Fig. 6 compares the delay distribution of the circuit before and  
after the optimisation procedure with AC, levelised AC with kꢀ=ꢀ3,  
and criticality metrics, after 15 rounds of optimisation, for C499,  
C1908, Arbiter, and Voter as a set of examples. As it can be seen,  
the mean and variance of the delay distribution of the circuits  
which are optimised with AC metric is smaller than the circuit  
optimised with the criticality concept. As a result, the TY of the  
circuit that is optimised with AC metric is more than the TY of the  
circuit optimised with the traditional criticality one. The results  
also show that the optimised delay distribution of the circuit using  
levelised criticality with kꢀ=ꢀ3 is very close to the delay distribution  
that is optimised with AC which shows the effectiveness of  
levelised AC metric.  
Figs. 7 and 8 compare the mean and variance of the circuit  
delay after each iteration of optimisation procedure for C499  
benchmark circuit using AC, levelised AC, and criticality metrics  
for 15 rounds of optimisation. As it can be seen, using AC concept,  
the mean and variance of the delay of the circuit are reduced the  
984  
IET Circuits Devices Syst., 2019, Vol. 13 Iss. 7, pp. 979-987  
© The Institution of Engineering and Technology 2019  
   
Fig. 8ꢁ Comparison of delay distribution of C499 benchmark circuit after each iteration of optimisation using:  
(a) levelised AC with kꢀ=ꢀ1, (b) levelised AC with kꢀ=ꢀ2, (c) levelised AC with kꢀ=ꢀ3, and (d) AC concept  
Fig. 9ꢁ Comparison of circuit delay distribution after optimisation with AC, levelised AC, and criticality concept under 10% area overhead constraint  
To compensate the runtime of the AC-based gate sizing  
algorithm, one could use the levelised AC-based gate sizing  
algorithm. As reported in Table 2, for kꢀ=ꢀ3, the runtime of each  
iteration of the levelised AC-based gate sizing algorithm is about  
406.12(s) which is 1.78× faster than the runtime of each iteration of  
the AC-based gate sizing algorithm. Furthermore, at each iteration,  
the levelised AC-based gate sizing algorithm improves the mean  
and variance of the circuit by 1.41% and 2.44%, respectively,  
which is close to the results obtained from the AC-based gate  
sizing algorithm.  
It is notable that choosing k for the levelised AC is a trade-off  
between runtime and accuracy. As shown in Table 2, by increasing  
k, the runtime of the algorithm increases (the runtime of each  
iteration of gate sizing algorithm based-on the levelised AC metric  
are 277.86(s) for kꢀ=ꢀ1, 325.31(s) for kꢀ=ꢀ2, and 406.12(s) for kꢀ=ꢀ3)  
but meanwhile, the mean and variance improvement of the  
optimisation algorithm become closer to the obtained results from  
the AC-based gate sizing optimisation, i.e. the average relative  
mean and variance improvement of each iteration of the levelised  
AC-based gate sizing algorithm are, respectively, 1.41 and 2.44%,

which are higher than the average relative mean and variance  
improvement of gate sizing based on the levelised AC with kꢀ=ꢀ1  
(0.86% mean improvement and 1.49% variance improvement) and

‘Iterations’ and ‘%Yield’ denote the number of iterations for  
convergence and the percentage of TY achieved by the sizing  
procedure, respectively. Column ‘%Area’ presents the percentage  
of area overhead imposed by the optimisation algorithm. The  
obtained results show that the proposed gate sizing method using  
AC concept can achieve the target yield constraint with only 2.79  
iterations and 10.02% area overhead, on average.  
5.2 Yield optimisation  
6ꢀConclusion  
In order to evaluate the convergence rate of the proposed gate  
sizing technique, we conduct an experiment where we use a  
dynamic threshold in which the gates with AC greater than a  
fraction (0.95) of the maximum AC (ACm ax) are chosen for  
optimisation, in every iteration. The algorithms stop when they  
achieve the target TY constraint which is 97% or the area overhead  
constraint which is 20% of initial circuit area is violated. Table 3  
shows the obtained results in which the columns named as  
Diagnostic metrics are the cornerstone of optimisation to provide  
valuable information for guiding statistical optimisation  
algorithms. In this paper, we introduce a novel optimisation metric  
called AC. Then, using the proposed metric, a gate sizing-based  
optimisation algorithm is presented that aims to improve the TY of  
a given circuit. Extensive experimental results are conducted to  
investigate the efficacy of the proposed metric with respect to the

TY improvement, area overhead, optimisation runtime, and  
convergence rate. Comparing to the conventional criticality metric,  
the obtained results show the superiority of the proposed AC  
metric when used as a diagnostic metric in a statistical optimisation  
algorithm in all aspects considered in the experiments.  
As discussed in [29], a change in the size of a gate increases the  
load capacitance seen by the gates in its fan-in cone. Therefore, it  
increases their delay and output slew. So, as a future extension of  
our work, the effect of fan-in gates on the AC of a gate should be  
taken into account.  
It is worthy to note that in this paper, we have assumed that the  
gates and circuit timing follow Gaussian distribution and thus, we  
use Gaussian distribution-based criticality computation method.  
However, the proposed AC metric is independent of the type of the  
distribution of random variables and one could use criticality  
computation methods presented in the [19, 21, 37] for computing  
criticality in digital circuits that does not follow the Gaussian  
distribution since these methods does not depend on any specific  
distributions or any particular delay models.
